results.possibleIdentity: Giselle Mauer (born 1930) is a Romanian-born sculptor and artist active during the late 1960s and early 1970s. She was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, a group founded to foster collaborations between the arts and sciences. In 1968, she participated in the E.A.T. exhibition "Some More Beginnings: An Exhibition of Submitted Works Involving Technical Materials and Processes" held at the Brooklyn Museum, where she collaborated with artist and physicist Ben-Ami Kadish. Her work often involved technical materials, including modern metal sculpture, one of which was unveiled at the Hillel Center in New Jersey in 1972. In 1966, she also organized an international art exhibition titled "Children of the World," which showcased children's artwork collected through cooperation with school teachers and ambassadors from various countries.

results.possibleIdentity: Ronald Mallory (June 17, 1932 – July 7, 2021) was an American architect and kinetic artist best known for his innovative 'mercury displacement' sculptures and paintings. Born in Philadelphia to Russian immigrant parents, Mallory initially studied architecture at the University of Florida and later worked in Brazil under modernist architect Oscar Niemeyer and landscape architect Roberto Burle Marx. Burle Marx encouraged him to pursue art, leading Mallory to relocate to Paris in the late 1950s, where he held early exhibitions, including a solo show at Galerie Iris Clert in 1961. Upon returning to New York, he shared a studio with the Belgian kinetic artist Pol Bury. Mallory gained significant acclaim in the mid-1960s for his kinetic mercury works—motor-driven acrylic or glass boxes containing liquid mercury that created fluid, shifting compositions. His debut solo exhibition for these works took place at the Stable Gallery in New York in 1966. He was actively associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg, and is documented within their historical archives. Throughout his career, he explored various mediums, including digital art, holograms, and jewelry design. His work is held in the permanent collections of major institutions such as the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the Whitney Museum of American Art, and the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art. He was married to actress and model Carole Mallory from 1968 to 1971 and spent his final years in San Miguel de Allende, Mexico.

results.possibleIdentity: Carl Andre (1935–2024) was an influential American Minimalist artist and sculptor, recognized as a defining figure in the movement. He was most active beginning in the 1960s and 1970s, during which he redefined sculpture by shifting it away from traditional carving and modeling toward the arrangement of raw industrial materials such as bricks, metal plates, timber, and stone. He famously described his work as "sculpture as place," creating low-lying, modular installations intended to be walked upon or experienced from within. Andre was associated with the circle surrounding Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) through his collaboration in the landmark 1966 event "9 Evenings: Theatre & Engineering," where he provided materials for Yvonne Rainer's choreographed performance "Carriage Discreteness." He was also a founding member of the Art Workers' Coalition in 1969. His career included major exhibitions at the Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum (1970) and the Whitney Museum. He is also noted for his involvement in a high-profile legal case following the 1985 death of his third wife, the artist Ana Mendieta, for which he was acquitted in 1988.
